title: Module 8: Confidence Scaling
learning_objective: Use confidence rulers to assess readiness for action; Explore what supports current confidence; Identify small steps that build confidence; Avoid over-reassurance or minimizing doubts
description: Learn to assess and explore confidence for weight loss changes using scaling questions
dialogue_content: {"nodes": [{"id": "conf_1", "patient_context": "Patient has expressed a specific goal", "patient_statement": "I want to walk for 30 minutes, 3 times a week. That's my goal.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "That's a great goal! You can definitely do this if you put your mind to it.", "feedback": "This cheerleading approach doesn't actually assess or build genuine confidence.", "technique": "Over-reassurance (ineffective)", "next_node_id": "conf_2a"}, {"text": "On a scale from 0 to 10, how confident do you feel about achieving that goal?", "feedback": "Perfect use of scaling to assess actual confidence level.", "technique": "Confidence scaling", "next_node_id": "conf_2b"}, {"text": "Walking 30 minutes is quite a lot if you're currently sedentary. Are you sure you can do that?", "feedback": "This introduces doubt instead of exploring the patient's actual confidence.", "technique": "Undermining confidence", "next_node_id": "conf_2c"}]}, {"id": "conf_2a", "patient_context": "Patient expresses doubt despite reassurance", "patient_statement": "I hope so. I've tried to start exercise programs before and they haven't worked out.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "You just need to believe in yourself more. You've got this!", "feedback": "This dismisses legitimate concerns and doesn't build real confidence.", "technique": "Continued over-reassurance", "next_node_id": "conf_end_poor"}, {"text": "Past attempts haven't gone as you'd hoped. On a scale of 0 to 10, how confident do you feel about this attempt?", "feedback": "Good - acknowledges the history and now assesses actual confidence.", "technique": "Reflection + Confidence scaling", "next_node_id": "conf_2b"}]}, {"id": "conf_2b", "patient_context": "Patient rates confidence at moderate level", "patient_statement": "Honestly? Maybe a 5. I want to do it, but I'm not sure I'll stick with it.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "A 5 is pretty low. You need to get that number up before you can start.", "feedback": "This judges the score negatively and creates pressure rather than exploration.", "technique": "Treating score as test/failure", "next_node_id": "conf_end_poor"}, {"text": "A 5 - so you have some confidence, but also some doubts. What makes it a 5 and not a 0?", "feedback": "Excellent - accepts the score and explores what confidence already exists.", "technique": "Exploring existing confidence", "next_node_id": "conf_3a"}, {"text": "Don't worry, your confidence will go up once you start seeing results.", "feedback": "This skips over the important work of understanding and building confidence now.", "technique": "Dismissing current confidence level", "next_node_id": "conf_2a"}]}, {"id": "conf_2c", "patient_context": "Patient second-guesses after practitioner doubt", "patient_statement": "Well, I'm not sure now. Maybe I should start with something smaller?", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "Let's think about what you actually feel confident about. On a scale of 0 to 10, how confident are you about taking some steps toward more activity?", "feedback": "Good recovery - moves away from doubt-inducing approach to assessment.", "technique": "Refocusing + Confidence scaling", "next_node_id": "conf_2b"}, {"text": "Yes, 30 minutes is probably too much. Let's aim for 10 minutes instead.", "feedback": "This takes over decision-making rather than exploring the patient's confidence.", "technique": "Imposing revised goal", "next_node_id": "conf_end_poor"}]}, {"id": "conf_3a", "patient_context": "Patient identifies sources of confidence", "patient_statement": "Well, I've got all the tools - a pair of legs, comfortable shoes. And I've done it before when I was younger. I know I can walk.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "So you have the physical ability and past success to draw on. What accounts for the other side - what makes it not an 8 or 9?", "feedback": "Perfect - affirms strengths and explores what's holding confidence back.", "technique": "Affirmation + Exploring barriers to higher confidence", "next_node_id": "conf_4a"}, {"text": "Right, so there's no reason you can't do it. You just need to start.", "feedback": "This dismisses the legitimate concerns that keep confidence at 5.", "technique": "Minimizing doubts", "next_node_id": "conf_end_poor"}, {"text": "Those are great resources. What's worked for you before when you've stayed active?", "feedback": "Good - builds on strengths and explores what has worked before.", "technique": "Affirmation + Exploring past success", "next_node_id": "conf_4b"}]}, {"id": "conf_4a", "patient_context": "Patient identifies confidence barrier", "patient_statement": "I guess it's the sticking with it part. I tend to start strong and then life gets in the way - work stress, family stuff. Then I stop.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "What would help move your confidence up even one point - say from a 5 to a 6?", "feedback": "Excellent - focuses on realistic, small improvements in confidence.", "technique": "Small increment scaling question", "next_node_id": "conf_5a"}, {"text": "You need to make exercise a priority. Put it in your calendar and treat it like any other appointment.", "feedback": "This jumps to solutions without exploring what would actually help the patient.", "technique": "Giving advice without permission", "next_node_id": "conf_end_poor"}, {"text": "Life gets in the way for everyone. You just have to push through it.", "feedback": "This minimizes real challenges instead of problem-solving collaboratively.", "technique": "Dismissing barriers", "next_node_id": "conf_end_poor"}]}, {"id": "conf_4b", "patient_context": "Patient recalls past success factor", "patient_statement": "When I was younger, I had friends who I'd work out with. We'd meet up and it was social, not just exercise. That made it fun.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "Having that social connection made a real difference. What might help recreate something like that now?", "feedback": "Good - honors past success and explores how to apply it now.", "technique": "Reflection + Open question", "next_node_id": "conf_5b"}, {"text": "Great! So you should find a walking buddy or join a group. Problem solved.", "feedback": "This rushes past the patient's own problem-solving process.", "technique": "Jumping to solution", "next_node_id": "conf_end_poor"}]}, {"id": "conf_5a", "patient_context": "Patient identifies confidence-building step", "patient_statement": "Maybe if I started with just 10 minutes instead of 30? That feels more doable. I could commit to that.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "Starting with 10 minutes is a smart way to build confidence. On that shorter walk goal, where's your confidence now?", "feedback": "Perfect - affirms the adaptive planning and reassesses confidence on the revised goal.", "technique": "Affirmation + Re-scaling", "next_node_id": "conf_end_good"}, {"text": "But 10 minutes won't really give you the health benefits you need. You should aim for at least 20.", "feedback": "This dismisses the patient's confidence-appropriate goal.", "technique": "Undermining patient's realistic assessment", "next_node_id": "conf_end_poor"}]}, {"id": "conf_5b", "patient_context": "Patient identifies support strategy", "patient_statement": "Actually, my neighbor mentioned she walks in the mornings. Maybe I could ask if she'd want company. That might help.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "That could provide the support and accountability you're looking for. How confident would you feel about reaching out to her?", "feedback": "Excellent - links to what matters and checks confidence on the specific action.", "technique": "Reflection + Confidence check on next step", "next_node_id": "conf_end_good"}, {"text": "Perfect! You should definitely do that. Having a walking partner is the key to success.", "feedback": "This pressures rather than supporting the patient's self-directed planning.", "technique": "Over-enthusiasm", "next_node_id": "conf_end_poor"}]}, {"id": "conf_end_good", "outcome": "Good engagement - Through thoughtful confidence scaling, the patient identified realistic steps that increased their confidence meaningfully.", "is_ending": true, "patient_context": "Successful confidence exploration and building", "learning_summary": "Confidence scaling involves asking the patient to rate their confidence on a scale (typically 0-10), then exploring what supports that confidence and what might increase it slightly. The focus is on understanding and building the patient's own assessment, not imposing external judgments about readiness.", "patient_statement": "With 10 minutes and maybe a walking buddy? Effective confidence work accepts the patient's rating, explores what gives them that confidence, and helps them identify realistic next steps.", "patient_statement": "I guess I'll try, but I'm not feeling great about it. Maybe I'm just not ready for this."}], "title": "Module 8: Confidence Scaling", "mi_process": "Focusing", "start_node": "conf_1", "description": "Learn to assess and explore confidence for weight loss changes using scaling questions", "stage_of_change": "Contemplation/Preparation", "technique_focus": "Confidence Scaling", "learning_objective": "Use confidence rulers to assess readiness for action; Explore what supports current confidence; Identify small steps that build confidence; Avoid over-reassurance or minimizing doubts"}

title: Module 11: Giving Information, Advice & Feedback
learning_objective: Ask permission before sharing information or advice; Build on what the person already knows; Keep information brief and neutral; Invite patient's interpretation
description: Learn to share information collaboratively using the Elicit-Provide-Elicit approach
dialogue_content: {"nodes": [{"id": "info_1", "patient_context": "Patient directly asks for information", "patient_statement": "I've been trying to lose weight, but I'm not sure I really understand what I need to do differently. What should I be doing?", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "You need to create a calorie deficit. That means eating less and exercising more. The evidence clearly shows that's the only way to lose weight.", "feedback": "This jumps straight to telling without first exploring what patient knows or asking permission.", "technique": "Lecturing without permission or eliciting", "next_node_id": "info_2a"}, {"text": "I'd be happy to share some information about that. First, what do you already know about weight loss - what have you heard or read?", "feedback": "Perfect - accepts the request and starts by eliciting what they already know.", "technique": "Elicit-Provide-Elicit (elicit phase)", "next_node_id": "info_2b"}, {"text": "It's really about willpower and discipline. If you're not losing weight, you're not trying hard enough.", "feedback": "This provides judgmental feedback that will likely create defensiveness.", "technique": "Judgmental advice-giving", "next_node_id": "info_2a"}]}, {"id": "info_2a", "patient_context": "Patient defensive after unsolicited advice", "patient_statement": "I know all that. It's not like I'm stupid. I just can't seem to make it work.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "I didn't say you were stupid. I'm just trying to help. You asked me what to do.", "feedback": "This becomes argumentative and damages the relationship further.", "technique": "Defensive response", "next_node_id": "info_end_poor"}, {"text": "You're right, I jumped in too quickly. You've already been working on this. What's your experience been so far with trying to lose weight?", "feedback": "Good recovery - practitioner takes responsibility and pivots to eliciting.", "technique": "Taking responsibility + Eliciting", "next_node_id": "info_2b"}]}, {"id": "info_2b", "patient_context": "Patient shares current knowledge and experience", "patient_statement": "Well, I know I need to eat less and move more. I've tried counting calories before. It worked for a while but then I got tired of tracking everything.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "Right, so you understand the basics and have experience with calorie tracking. Would it be okay if I shared some information about different approaches that might fit better with your lifestyle?", "feedback": "Excellent - acknowledges what they know and explicitly asks permission before providing information.", "technique": "Affirming knowledge + Asking permission (provide phase)", "next_node_id": "info_3a"}, {"text": "Well, you need to keep tracking. Most people who stop tracking regain the weight. That's just how it works.", "feedback": "This gives directive advice without permission or exploring alternatives.", "technique": "Prescriptive without permission", "next_node_id": "info_2a"}, {"text": "You're obviously not seeing the bigger picture. Let me explain the science to you.", "feedback": "This belittles the patient and takes an expert-superior position.", "technique": "Condescending stance", "next_node_id": "info_end_poor"}]}, {"id": "info_3a", "patient_context": "Patient gives permission for information", "patient_statement": "Sure, I'm open to hearing about different approaches.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "One thing some people find helpful is focusing on food quality rather than just quantity - like emphasizing whole foods, vegetables, lean proteins. Others find success with pattern changes like eating at regular times or reducing portions gradually rather than tracking every calorie. Some combine small changes in eating with increased activity. Different approaches work for different people.", "feedback": "Good - provides information neutrally, briefly, and presents options rather than directives.", "technique": "Providing neutral options briefly (provide phase)", "next_node_id": "info_4a"}, {"text": "Well, the research shows that calorie tracking is the most effective method. People who track consistently lose the most weight and keep it off. You really should give it another try but this time stick with it. Here are all the apps I recommend, and let me explain how to calculate your basal metabolic rate...", "feedback": "This provides too much information, is overly prescriptive, and doesn't check understanding.", "technique": "Overloading with information + Prescriptive", "next_node_id": "info_end_poor"}]}, {"id": "info_4a", "patient_context": "Patient responds to information", "patient_statement": "That's interesting. I hadn't thought about the food quality approach instead of just counting.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "What do you make of that idea? How does that land with you?", "feedback": "Perfect - invites the patient's interpretation and returns control to them.", "technique": "Inviting interpretation (elicit phase)", "next_node_id": "info_5a"}, {"text": "Yes, food quality is definitely the best approach. That's what you should focus on.", "feedback": "This tells the patient what to think rather than eliciting their perspective.", "technique": "Directing interpretation", "next_node_id": "info_2a"}, {"text": "There's a lot more I need to tell you about nutrition science. Let me explain macros and micronutrients...", "feedback": "This dumps more information without checking how the first information was received.", "technique": "Continuing to overload without checking", "next_node_id": "info_end_poor"}]}, {"id": "info_5a", "patient_context": "Patient interprets and applies information", "patient_statement": "It sounds more doable than tracking everything. Like, I could focus on adding more vegetables and choosing grilled chicken instead of fried, that kind of thing?", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "You're already thinking about specific, practical ways you could apply that approach. What appeals to you about that way of thinking about food?", "feedback": "Excellent - affirms patient's application and continues exploring their perspective.", "technique": "Affirmation + Continued eliciting", "next_node_id": "info_end_good"}, {"text": "Well, you'd also need to watch portions, avoid processed foods, eliminate added sugars, increase fiber...", "feedback": "This loads on more requirements instead of staying with the patient's understanding.", "technique": "Adding requirements without checking", "next_node_id": "info_end_poor"}]}, {"id": "info_end_good", "outcome": "Good engagement - Using Elicit-Provide-Elicit, information was shared collaboratively and the patient integrated it in their own way.", "is_ending": true, "patient_context": "Successful information exchange", "learning_summary": "The Elicit-Provide-Elicit approach transforms information-giving from a lecture to a collaborative exchange: (1) Elicit what the person already knows, (2) Ask permission and provide brief, neutral information, (3) Elicit their interpretation and application. This builds partnership and increases the likelihood that information will be used.", "patient_statement": "I think it's less overwhelming. With tracking, I felt like I was obsessing over every calorie. This feels more like I could just make better choices without the constant math. I think I could try that."}, {"id": "info_end_poor", "outcome": "Poor engagement - Giving unsolicited advice, overloading with information, or being prescriptive led to confusion and disengagement.", "is_ending": true, "patient_context": "Poor outcome from information overload or lecture", "learning_summary": "Giving unsolicited advice, lecturing without first eliciting, overloading with too much information, being prescriptive rather than presenting options, and failing to check the patient's interpretation all undermine effective information exchange in MI.", "patient_statement": "I feel more confused now than when I started. Maybe I should just forget about it."}], "title": "Module 11: Giving Information, Advice & Feedback", "mi_process": "Engaging/Focusing", "start_node": "info_1", "description": "Learn to share information collaboratively using the Elicit-Provide-Elicit approach", "stage_of_change": "Contemplation/Preparation", "technique_focus": "Elicit-Provide-Elicit", "learning_objective": "Ask permission before sharing information or advice; Build on what the person already knows; Keep information brief and neutral; Invite patient's interpretation"}

title: Module 1: Simple Reflections - Building Engagement
learning_objective: Learn to use simple reflections to demonstrate understanding and build rapport with a patient in precontemplation
description: Simple reflections repeat or rephrase what the patient said to show you're listening and understand. They build trust and encourage the patient to continue exploring.
dialogue_content: {"nodes": [{"id": "node_1", "patient_context": "Precontemplation - Patient doesn't see smoking as a problem and may feel coerced into the conversation", "patient_statement": "My doctor sent me here to talk about quitting smoking, but honestly, I don't see what the big deal is. I've been smoking for 20 years and I'm fine.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "Your doctor is concerned about your smoking.", "feedback": "This reflects only part of what the patient said - you missed their perspective that they don't see it as a problem. A more complete reflection would acknowledge their view.", "technique": "Simple reflection (partial)", "next_node_id": "node_2a"}, {"text": "You don't see your smoking as a problem right now.", "feedback": "Excellent! This simple reflection captures the patient's perspective without judgment. It shows you heard them and creates space for them to continue.", "technique": "Simple reflection (complete)", "next_node_id": "node_2b"}, {"text": "Even though you've been smoking for 20 years, it's actually causing damage you can't see yet.", "feedback": "This contradicts the patient and jumps to educating them. The 'righting reflex' often increases resistance in precontemplation. Try reflecting their perspective first.", "technique": "Righting reflex (non-MI)", "next_node_id": "node_2c"}]}, {"id": "node_2a", "patient_context": "Patient continues to minimize concerns", "patient_statement": "Yeah, she is. But she worries about everything. I feel fine, my breathing is okay. I don't get what everyone's so worried about.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "From your perspective, everything feels fine with your health.", "feedback": "Good reflection! You're continuing to show understanding of their perspective, which builds rapport even when they're resistant.", "technique": "Simple reflection", "next_node_id": "node_3a"}, {"text": "Your breathing feels okay to you right now.", "feedback": "Excellent! This reflects a specific detail the patient mentioned, showing you're listening carefully. Specificity strengthens the reflection.", "technique": "Simple reflection (specific)", "next_node_id": "node_3a"}, {"text": "But are you sure your breathing is really okay? Sometimes people don't notice gradual changes.", "feedback": "This challenges the patient's perception and can increase defensiveness. In precontemplation, reflecting their view builds more rapport than questioning it.", "technique": "Questioning/challenging (non-MI)", "next_node_id": "node_3b"}]}, {"id": "node_2b", "patient_context": "Patient feels validated and continues to share their perspective", "patient_statement": "Exactly! I mean, if I had a problem, I'd know it, right? I can still do everything I used to do.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "You feel like you'd notice if smoking was affecting you.", "feedback": "Perfect! This reflection captures their reasoning and continues to build understanding. You're resisting the urge to correct or educate.", "technique": "Simple reflection", "next_node_id": "node_3c"}, {"text": "You can still do everything you used to do.", "feedback": "Good! This is a very literal reflection. While accurate, the previous option added slightly more depth by capturing the underlying belief.", "technique": "Simple reflection (literal)", "next_node_id": "node_3c"}, {"text": "Actually, many smokers don't realize they're being affected until serious damage has occurred.", "feedback": "This is the 'righting reflex' - jumping to educate. While factually correct, it breaks rapport and increases resistance. Stay with reflections in early engagement.", "technique": "Educating/righting reflex (non-MI)", "next_node_id": "node_3b"}]}, {"id": "node_2c", "patient_context": "Patient is becoming resistant due to feeling lectured", "patient_statement": "Look, I appreciate you trying to help, but I didn't come here to be lectured. I get enough of that from my family.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "You're feeling lectured, and that's frustrating for you.", "feedback": "Excellent recovery! Reflecting their emotion (frustration) shows empathy and can help repair the rapport. This is 'rolling with resistance.'", "technique": "Simple reflection (emotion)", "next_node_id": "node_3d"}, {"text": "I'm not here to lecture you. I genuinely want to help you be healthier.", "feedback": "Defending yourself can make things worse. Instead, reflect the patient's experience to show you understand their frustration.", "technique": "Defending/explaining (non-MI)", "next_node_id": "node_end_resistance"}]}, {"id": "node_3a", "patient_context": "Patient mentions a symptom but normalizes it", "patient_statement": "Right. I mean, sure, I cough in the morning sometimes, but that's normal for smokers, isn't it?", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "You notice some coughing, and you see that as just part of being a smoker.", "feedback": "Excellent! This reflects both parts - the symptom and their interpretation - without judgment. You're building a complete picture of their perspective.", "technique": "Simple reflection (neutral)", "next_node_id": "node_4a"}, {"text": "You cough in the mornings.", "feedback": "This is accurate but misses the patient's interpretation (that they see it as normal). The more complete reflection captures their full meaning.", "technique": "Simple reflection (minimal)", "next_node_id": "node_4a"}, {"text": "That morning cough is actually called 'smoker's cough' and it means your lungs are trying to clear out tar and toxins.", "feedback": "While educational, this breaks the engagement process. You're still building rapport - save education for later when they're more open to it.", "technique": "Educating (premature)", "next_node_id": "node_3b"}]}, {"id": "node_3b", "outcome": "Poor engagement - Patient has withdrawn from the conversation", "is_ending": true, "patient_context": "Patient is disengaging due to feeling pressured or challenged", "learning_summary": "When we challenge, educate, or contradict a patient in precontemplation, we often increase resistance rather than build motivation. Simple reflections that demonstrate understanding - even when we disagree - build the foundation for future change conversations.", "patient_statement": "You know what, I don't think this is for me. Thanks anyway."}, {"id": "node_3c", "patient_context": "Patient provides evidence for their view", "patient_statement": "Yeah. I walk my dog every day, I work a physical job. If smoking was really hurting me, I couldn't do those things.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "Your daily activities - walking your dog, your physical job - those are evidence to you that you're doing okay.", "feedback": "Perfect! This reflection amplifies what they said, showing you really understand their reasoning. This builds strong rapport.", "technique": "Simple reflection (amplifying)", "next_node_id": "node_4b"}, {"text": "You work a physical job.", "feedback": "Accurate but incomplete. You captured one detail but missed the connection they're making between their activities and their health assessment.", "technique": "Simple reflection (incomplete)", "next_node_id": "node_4b"}]}, {"id": "node_3d", "patient_context": "Patient feels their autonomy is being violated", "patient_statement": "Yeah, it is. Everyone acts like they know what's best for me better than I do.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "You feel like people aren't respecting that you know yourself best.", "feedback": "Excellent! This reflection captures the deeper meaning - their need for autonomy and respect. This kind of reflection deepens rapport.", "technique": "Simple reflection (underlying meaning)", "next_node_id": "node_4c"}, {"text": "People are telling you what to do.", "feedback": "Accurate but stays at the surface. The previous option captures the emotional impact - feeling disrespected - which shows deeper empathy.", "technique": "Simple reflection (surface level)", "next_node_id": "node_4c"}]}, {"id": "node_4a", "patient_context": "Patient shares family history as evidence", "patient_statement": "Exactly. My dad smoked till he was 75 and lived a good life. I figure genetics matter more than people think.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "Your dad's experience - smoking and still living to 75 - that shapes how you think about risk.", "feedback": "Excellent! This reflection shows you understand where their beliefs come from. You've built solid rapport through consistent reflection.", "technique": "Simple reflection (contextual)", "next_node_id": "node_end_good"}, {"text": "You think genetics are more important than smoking.", "feedback": "Good reflection. Both options are MI-adherent; the first adds slightly more context about why they believe this.", "technique": "Simple reflection", "next_node_id": "node_end_good"}]}, {"id": "node_4b", "patient_context": "Patient feels there's a disconnect between medical advice and their lived experience", "patient_statement": "Right. So when my doctor gets all serious about it, it's hard to take seriously, you know?", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "There's a gap between what your doctor is saying and what you're experiencing in your body.", "feedback": "Perfect! This reflection identifies the discrepancy they're experiencing. You've demonstrated deep understanding throughout this conversation.", "technique": "Simple reflection (identifying discrepancy)", "next_node_id": "node_end_good"}, {"text": "It's hard to take your doctor seriously about this.", "feedback": "Good reflection! Both options work well here - the first adds a bit more depth about why it's hard to take seriously.", "technique": "Simple reflection", "next_node_id": "node_end_good"}]}, {"id": "node_4c", "patient_context": "Patient appreciates feeling heard and understood", "patient_statement": "Thanks for understanding that. I guess I'm just tired of feeling judged, you know?", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "You're tired of feeling judged about your smoking.", "feedback": "Excellent! By consistently reflecting without judgment, you've repaired the relationship and created an opening for future conversations.", "technique": "Simple reflection", "next_node_id": "node_end_good"}]}, {"id": "node_end_good"...

title: Module 7: Collaborative Climate & Focusing
learning_objective: Create a collaborative rather than authoritative conversational climate; Invite the patient to set priorities collaboratively; Use transparency about time and structure; Balance flexibility with focus
description: Learn to establish a collaborative partnership and set a shared agenda at the start of conversations about weight loss
dialogue_content: {"nodes": [{"id": "collab_1", "patient_context": "Initial conversation about weight and activity level", "patient_statement": "Well, I sit at a desk all day for work. By evening I'm too tired to do anything except watch TV. I used to be more active when I was younger.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "You need to start exercising at least 30 minutes a day and cut your calorie intake by 500 calories.", "feedback": "This directive approach ignores the patient's perspective and imposes an agenda, likely increasing resistance.", "technique": "Righting reflex (ineffective)", "next_node_id": "collab_2a"}, {"text": "You notice quite a contrast between your current lifestyle and how you used to be. What would be most useful to focus on today?", "feedback": "This validates the patient's observation and invites them to set priorities, creating a collaborative climate.", "technique": "Reflection + Collaborative agenda setting", "next_node_id": "collab_2b"}, {"text": "Let me explain the health risks of being sedentary and overweight. It's very important you understand this.", "feedback": "This takes an expert stance that may feel patronizing and doesn't honor patient autonomy.", "technique": "Education/Lecturing (ineffective)", "next_node_id": "collab_2c"}]}, {"id": "collab_2a", "patient_context": "Patient resistance after directive approach", "patient_statement": "That sounds like a lot. I don't think I can do all that right now. I barely have time as it is.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "If you don't make time, your health will continue to decline. It's really up to you.", "feedback": "This sounds like a threat disguised as respecting choice, damaging the relationship.", "technique": "Righting reflex with false autonomy", "next_node_id": "collab_end_poor"}, {"text": "I hear that feels overwhelming. Let's step back - what matters most to you about your health right now?", "feedback": "Good recovery - acknowledging the discord and returning to collaboration.", "technique": "Rolling with discord + Refocusing collaboratively", "next_node_id": "collab_3b"}]}, {"id": "collab_2b", "patient_context": "Patient responds to collaborative invitation", "patient_statement": "I guess I'm most worried about my weight and how tired I feel all the time. But I'm not sure where to start.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "Those are connected concerns. We have about 15 minutes today - would it help to explore what might be behind that fatigue?", "feedback": "Excellent - makes connections, is transparent about time, and seeks permission to focus.", "technique": "Linking + Transparent structure + Permission", "next_node_id": "collab_3c"}, {"text": "The fatigue is probably from being overweight. title: Module 12: Anticipatory Coping & Relapse Prevention
learning_objective: Identify situations that might make change harder; Create flexible coping responses; Normalize lapses and focus on learning; Separate behavior from identity
description: Learn to prepare patients for challenges and normalize setbacks as part of the change process
dialogue_content: {"nodes": [{"id": "anti_1", "patient_context": "Patient reports success with initial change", "patient_statement": "I've been walking regularly for a few weeks now and it's going well. Maintenance is when most people slip up. You need to be realistic.", "feedback": "This reinforces discouragement rather than building coping skills.", "technique": "Doubling down on pessimism", "next_node_id": "anti_end_poor"}]}, {"id": "anti_2b", "patient_context": "Patient identifies realistic challenges", "patient_statement": "Well, the weather is getting colder. What has helped you in the past when the weather was bad or you had a busy schedule?", "feedback": "Excellent - normalizes challenges and explores patient's existing coping resources.", "technique": "Normalizing + Drawing on past coping", "next_node_id": "anti_3a"}, {"text": "Don't let cold weather stop you. title: Module 5: Summarizing - Linking and Transitioning
learning_objective: Learn to create effective summaries that gather discussions, highlight key themes, and facilitate transitions between MI processes
description: Summaries pull together what's been discussed, demonstrate listening, and create natural transitions. Effective summaries are: (1) Collecting - gathering what's been said, (2) Linking - connecting themes, (3) Transitional - moving to next steps. They can strategically emphasize change talk and are offered with humility ('Let me see if I have this right...').
dialogue_content: {"nodes": [{"id": "node_1", "patient_context": "Patient has shared multiple elements: ambivalence, motivation (granddaughter), barrier (work stress), action taken (research), and concern (complexity). This is a good moment for a summary.", "patient_statement": "So yeah, I've been going back and forth on this smoking thing for weeks now. My granddaughter's three months old already. Patches, gum, that prescription medication. I don't know... it all seems complicated.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "You're stressed about quitting and it seems complicated.", "feedback": "This captures only a small part of what they shared and focuses on the negative/sustain talk side. A more complete summary would gather all the elements they mentioned.", "technique": "Minimal summary (incomplete)", "next_node_id": "node_2a"}, {"text": "Let me see if I'm following you. You're experiencing this pull in different directions - when you're with your granddaughter, quitting feels important and right. At work, especially under stress, smoking feels necessary. You've taken the step of researching methods, and now you're weighing the complexity of the different options. How am I doing?", "feedback": "Excellent! This collecting summary gathers the key elements: ambivalence, specific contexts, action taken, and current concerns. Asking 'How am I doing?' invites correction and shows humility.", "technique": "Collecting summary (comprehensive)", "next_node_id": "node_2b"}, {"text": "You want to quit for your granddaughter but work stress makes it hard, and you've researched some methods.", "feedback": "This is accurate and includes key elements, but it's somewhat flat and list-like. The previous option has more depth and uses language that reflects the emotional quality of their experience.", "technique": "Simple summary (adequate but flat)", "next_node_id": "node_2c"}]}, {"id": "node_2a", "patient_context": "Patient corrects incomplete summary that emphasized sustain talk", "patient_statement": "Well, it's not just that. I mean, the stress is real, but so is wanting to be around for my granddaughter. You're making it sound like I'm just focused on the negatives.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "You're absolutely right - I missed the big picture there. Let me try again. You're pulled between two strong forces: your granddaughter, who makes you want to quit, and work stress, where smoking feels like a necessary coping tool. And you've been actively researching options even while feeling uncertain. Is that more complete?", "feedback": "Excellent recovery! You acknowledged the incomplete summary and offered a more balanced one that includes both change talk and sustain talk. When a patient says you missed something, believe them and adjust.", "technique": "Defending incomplete summary (non-MI)", "next_node_id": "node_3b"}]}, {"id": "node_2b", "patient_context": "Patient confirms summary and adds new information about waiting/readiness", "patient_statement": "Yeah, that's pretty much it. I feel like I'm in limbo. I know I need to make a decision, but I keep waiting for the 'right moment' or for it to feel easier.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "So you're in this in-between place - you know a decision is needed, but you're waiting for conditions that would make it feel more manageable. There's a gap between knowing and being ready.", "feedback": "Excellent! This linking summary connects the concepts they've shared - the in-between place, the need for decision, the waiting, and the gap between knowing and readiness. This shows deeper understanding.", "technique": "Linking summary (connecting themes)", "next_node_id": "node_3c"}, {"text": "You're waiting for the right moment. What would that look like?", "feedback": "This is MI-adherent and the question could be useful. However, you just received confirmation of a complex situation - a summary that links themes would demonstrate deeper understanding before moving to questions.", "technique": "Simple reflection + open question", "next_node_id": "node_3d"}, {"text": "There's never going to be a perfect moment. You just have to decide and do it.", "feedback": "This is the righting reflex - telling them what they need to do. It dismisses their experience and creates pressure. It's frustrating being stuck like this. Part of me thinks I should just pick a method and try, but another part says I'm not ready.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "You're experiencing an internal tug-of-war - one voice says 'just try something' and another says 'not yet.' And underneath that, there's frustration with being in this stuck place.", "feedback": "Excellent! This linking summary names the internal conflict and connects it to the emotion (frustration). This shows you understand the complexity of their experience.", "technique": "Linking summary (internal conflict)", "next_node_id": "node_3f"}, {"text": "Part of you wants to try and part of you doesn't feel ready.", "feedback": "This is a good double-sided reflection and is MI-adherent. However, since they've now shared quite a bit, a fuller summary might demonstrate more complete listening than another single reflection.", "technique": "Double-sided reflection", "next_node_id": "node_3g"}]}, {"id": "node_3a", "patient_context": "Patient elaborates on the challenge of too many conflicting opinions", "patient_statement": "Yes, that's much better. title: Module 9: Decisional Balance
learning_objective: Explore pros and cons of both changing and staying the same; Give equal attention to both sides of ambivalence; Avoid using decisional balance to persuade; Let the patient draw their own conclusions
description: Learn to explore ambivalence by examining both sides of change decisions about weight loss

title: Module 6: Recognizing and Evoking Change Talk (DARN-CAT)
learning_objective: Learn to recognize different types of change talk, selectively reflect change talk to strengthen it, and use strategic questions to evoke change talk across the DARN-CAT spectrum
description: Change talk is any patient statement about desire, ability, reasons, or need for change (DARN - preparatory change talk) or commitment, activation, or taking steps (CAT - mobilizing change talk). Skilled practitioners recognize change talk, reflect it to strengthen it, and use strategic questions to evoke it. This shifts the balance from ambivalence toward change.

title: Module 3: Complex & Double-Sided Reflections - Exploring Ambivalence
learning_objective: Learn to use complex reflections to capture deeper meaning and double-sided reflections to acknowledge ambivalence without pushing for resolution
description: Complex reflections add meaning, emotion, or deeper understanding beyond what the patient explicitly said. Double-sided reflections acknowledge both sides of ambivalence (reasons to change AND reasons to stay the same), helping patients hold their complexity without feeling pushed.

But I've been smoking for 25 years - it's part of who I am.", "practitioner_choices": [{"text": "Your daughter's pregnancy is making you think about quitting.", "feedback": "This is accurate but only reflects one side - the reason to change. title: Module 10: Planning & Implementation Intentions
learning_objective: Check readiness before moving to planning; Elicit the patient's ideas for plans first; Use if-then implementation intentions; Link plans to existing routines and check confidence
description: Learn to help patients create specific, realistic action plans using implementation intentions

title: Module 2: Open-Ended Questions - Inviting Exploration
learning_objective: Learn to ask open-ended questions that invite patients to explore their thoughts and feelings, rather than closed questions that limit responses
description: Open-ended questions invite elaboration and exploration. They typically start with 'What,' 'How,' or 'Tell me about...' rather than requiring yes/no answers. In MI, we follow the general rule of asking one open question followed by 2-3 reflections.

title: Module 4: Affirmations - Recognizing Strengths Without Cheerleading
learning_objective: Learn to offer genuine affirmations that recognize patient strengths, efforts, and values without cheerleading, premature reassurance, or empty praise
description: Affirmations recognize and amplify patient strengths, efforts, and positive qualities. Genuine affirmations are specific, sincere, and build self-efficacy. They differ from cheerleading (overly enthusiastic), false reassurance ('I'm sure you can do it'), or flattery (generic praise). Good affirmations notice what's already there.
